common_name: White Acre Pea (Sweet Small-Seeded Gulf South Cream Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; White Acre pea is heat-tolerant and thrives in Gulf South summer heat — plant succession crops through summer for continuous fresh production from May through September in zone 9.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; White Acre pea is one of the most distinctive and prized southern pea varieties of the Texas and Gulf Coast tradition — a small-seeded cream pea producing petite, white to cream-white seeds with exceptionally sweet, buttery, delicate flavor that differs meaningfully from the larger-seeded, more robust flavor of standard cream peas and blackeye peas. The small seed size of White Acre is characteristic — the peas are notably smaller than regular cream peas, more compact in the shelled bowl, and cook to a sweeter, creamier texture with a refined flavor profile that has made White Acre the preference of many Gulf South cooks for fresh summer pea dishes. In zone 9, White Acre produces abundantly from its first harvest through summer and into fall — planting succession crops every 3–4 weeks from April through August provides continuous fresh pea production throughout the longest part of the growing season. White Acre is a semi-bush to vining type reaching 18–24 inches and performs well without staking in most garden conditions. The vines fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility for subsequent crops. Harvest when hulls are well-filled but still green for fresh-shelled use; allow to dry completely for storage. White Acre is particularly important in Gulf Coast cuisine and is a foundational variety of the traditional summer pea dishes of Louisiana, Texas, and Mississippi.

common_name: Pink Eye Purple Hull Pea (Classic Gulf South Dual-Color Summer Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pink Eye Purple Hull peas are highly heat-tolerant — they thrive and produce abundantly in Gulf South summer heat that limits production of most other vegetables.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pink Eye Purple Hull is one of the most widely grown and deeply traditional southern pea varieties of the Gulf South — a semi-bush to vining type producing distinctive purple-hulled pods that make harvest timing and identification visually clear (green hulls shade to purple as the peas approach maturity), with cream-white peas marked by a distinctive pink to rose-colored 'eye' or hilum that gives the variety its evocative name. The pink eye is the identifying mark: when a pot of fresh-shelled Pink Eye Purple Hull peas is spread on the counter, the cream peas with their small pink-rose eye marks are immediately recognizable as this specific variety. The flavor is excellent — classic southern pea quality with a rich, earthy, beany sweetness that is characteristic of the Gulf South summer pea tradition. Pink Eye Purple Hull is extremely productive in Gulf South conditions, continuing to produce through summer heat that limits most other garden vegetables. It is among the most important vegetable crops of the traditional Gulf South summer garden, providing fresh food through July, August, and September when garden production from other crops has ended. Nitrogen-fixing root system improves soil. The purple hulls are ornamentally attractive in the garden and make identifying ripe pods easy and unambiguous.

common_name: Champion Collards (Upright Heat-Adapted Medium-Leaf Gulf South Collard)

planting.note: Direct sow 10–12 weeks before the first frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0) soil in full sun; in zone 9, Champion Collards can also be sown in late January–February for a spring harvest, though the fall/winter crop produces the sweetest, most tender greens.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Champion is a widely recommended collard variety for Gulf South home gardens — producing upright plants with medium to large, waxy, blue-green leaves that are somewhat more compact and manageable than old-fashioned large-leaf types. Champion is valued for its combination of good heat tolerance in the planting period (August–September establishment in zone 9 requires tolerance of remaining summer heat), productive fall and winter yields, and excellent flavor quality that improves after frost exposure — the cold sweetens collard leaves by converting starches to sugars, a phenomenon well-known to Gulf South gardeners who prefer the post-frost harvest for their finest-flavored greens. The waxy leaf surface of Champion reduces water loss and contributes to the heat-establishment tolerance. In zone 9, collards are grown as a cool-season crop planted in August–September and harvested through November–February; the mild Gulf South winter allows continuous harvest from established plants without the repeated freeze damage that ends collard production in northern climates. Harvest outer leaves progressively (leaving the growing tip intact) for extended production from each plant. Champion has been recommended by southern cooperative extension services for decades as a reliable, productive, good-quality collard for both home garden and small-farm production.

common_name: Lemon Queen Swamp Sunflower (Pale-Yellow Compact Native Fall Perennial)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Lemon Queen blooms in fall (September–November) and develops its full height by mid-summer — place toward the back of borders where the tall stems are supported by adjacent plantings.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Lemon Queen is a compact cultivar of swamp sunflower (Helianthus angustifolius) producing pale-yellow, lemon-colored flowers rather than the golden-yellow of the species — a softer, cooler yellow that creates a different aesthetic quality in the fall garden. The pale lemon-yellow flowers with dark brown centers bloom in September–November when few other perennials are at peak display, filling the fall garden with a naturalistic cloud of soft-yellow daisy-form flowers that are outstanding for cut flowers and highly attractive to migrating monarch butterflies, native bees, and seed-eating birds. 'Lemon Queen' is somewhat more compact than the straight species (4–6 feet rather than 6–10 feet for vigorous species plants) and less likely to require staking — a practical advantage for typical residential borders where the species' tall, floppy stems can be problematic. The pale yellow flower color allows combination with blue-purple asters, violet-flowered plants (ironweed, ageratum), and warm orange-red tones (ornamental grasses, late salvias) in fall combinations without the color intensity of the golden-yellow species. Division every 3–4 years in spring prevents center die-out in mature clumps. An outstanding choice for rain gardens, moist spots, and naturalistic borders where fall native color and pollinator support are priorities.

common_name: Thunder Cloud Texas Sage (Deep-Purple Compact Cenizo Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to dry, alkaline to neutral (pH 6.5–8.0), lean soil in full sun; Thunder Cloud absolutely requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate standing water or heavy wet clay, and root rot in poorly drained soil is the primary cause of failure in humid Gulf South gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Thunder Cloud is one of the most compact and richly colored Texas sage (cenizo) cultivars — producing the species' characteristic silvery-gray, small, soft-textured foliage on a more restrained 3–4 foot plant, with deep purple-violet flowers that are more saturated and darker than the typical medium-purple of the species and many other cultivars. The compact habit makes Thunder Cloud proportionate for typical residential borders and containers where the sprawling, larger selections would overgrow the planting space. Like all cenizo, Thunder Cloud blooms in remarkable, synchronized flushes triggered by periods of high humidity or moisture after dry weather — the sudden appearance of masses of purple flowers across an entire planting following a rain or fog event is one of the most dramatic of Gulf South garden phenomena. The silvery foliage is attractive year-round — the pale gray-silver leaf color provides an effective contrast to green or dark foliage companions. Leucophyllum requires excellent drainage — in the humid Gulf South east of San Antonio/Houston, site in raised beds, berms, or rocky slopes where water moves away from roots quickly. Once established in appropriate drainage conditions, Thunder Cloud requires virtually no supplemental watering or fertilization. An outstanding choice for xeriscaping, reflected-heat positions, and caliche/alkaline soils where other ornamental shrubs struggle.
